stem: A function \( f \) has period [[T]]; that is, \( f(x + [[T]]) = f(x) \) for all \( x \). On one period, for \( 0 \le x < [[T]] \), it is given by \( f(x) = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \). What is \( f([[x1]]) \)?
solution: By periodicity, reduce the input modulo [[T]]: \( [[x1]] \equiv [[r]] \pmod{[[T]]} \) with \( 0 \le [[r]] < [[T]] \). Then \( f([[x1]]) = f([[r]]) = [[a]]([[r]]) + ([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This plugs \( [[x1]] \) straight into the one-period formula without reducing modulo [[T]] first.
This reflects the remainder as \( [[T]] - r \) instead of using \( r \) itself.
This uses the number of whole periods \( \lfloor [[x1]]/[[T]] \rfloor \) in place of the remainder.
This uses the correct remainder but drops the constant term \( ([[b]]) \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The function \( f \) is even; that is, \( f(-x) = f(x) \) for all \( x \). For \( x \ge 0 \), \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). What is \( f(-[[x0]]) \)?
solution: Since \( f \) is even, \( f(-[[x0]]) = f([[x0]]) \). Substituting into the given formula (valid for \( x \ge 0 \)): \( f([[x0]]) = [[a]]([[x0]])^{2} + ([[b]])([[x0]]) + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This negates the value, as if \( f \) were odd rather than even.
This flips the sign of only the linear term, as if substituting \( -[[x0]] \) directly into the formula meant for \( x \ge 0 \).
This drops the linear term entirely instead of substituting \( [[x0]] \) for \( x \).
This flips the sign of the quadratic and constant terms but not the linear one, an inconsistent sign error.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The function \( f \) is odd; that is, \( f(-x) = -f(x) \) for all \( x \). For \( x \ge 0 \), \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{3} + ([[b]])x \). What is \( f(-[[x0]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This treats \( f \) as even instead of odd, forgetting to negate the value.
This flips the sign of only the cubic term instead of the whole expression.
This flips the sign of only the linear term instead of the whole expression.
This computes \( -f([[x0]]) \) with both terms independently negated as if squared, a sign-distribution slip that happens to double-negate incorrectly for this odd/even mix.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Suppose \( f \) is even and \( g \) is odd, with \( f([[p]]) = [[A]] \) and \( g([[p]]) = [[B]] \). Let \( h(x) = f(x) \cdot g(x) \). What is \( h(-[[p]]) \)?
solution: Since \( f \) is even, \( f(-[[p]]) = f([[p]]) = [[A]] \). Since \( g \) is odd, \( g(-[[p]]) = -g([[p]]) = -[[B]] \). So \( h(-[[p]]) = f(-[[p]]) \cdot g(-[[p]]) = ([[A]])(-[[B]]) = [[answer]] \); the product of an even and an odd function is always odd.
distractor_rationales: This treats \( h \) as even, forgetting that the product of an even and an odd function is odd.
This subtracts the two given values instead of multiplying them (and gets the sign wrong).
This negates both factors instead of just the odd one.
This adds the two given values instead of multiplying them.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The function \( f \) has period [[T]]. Define \( g(x) = f\!\left(\dfrac{x}{[[k]]}\right) \). What is the period of \( g \)?
solution: Replacing \( x \) with \( x/[[k]] \) stretches the graph horizontally by a factor of [[k]], so the period scales by the same factor: the period of \( g \) is \( [[T]] \cdot [[k]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( [[k]] \) instead of multiplying, as if the graph were compressed rather than stretched.
This ignores the horizontal stretch entirely and reuses \( f \)'s original period.
This adds the stretch factor to the period instead of multiplying by it.
This subtracts the stretch factor from the period instead of multiplying by it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The graph of \( f \) is symmetric about the vertical line \( x = [[h]] \); that is, \( f([[h]] + x) = f([[h]] - x) \) for all \( x \). Given that \( f([[p]]) = f(q) \) for some \( q \ne [[p]] \), what is \( q \)?
solution: Symmetry about \( x = [[h]] \) means \( q \) is the mirror image of \( [[p]] \) across \( x = [[h]] \): \( q = [[h]] + ([[h]] - [[p]]) = 2([[h]]) - [[p]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to double the axis of symmetry \( [[h]] \) when reflecting.
This negates the correct value (a sign slip in the reflection formula).
This adds instead of reflecting, using \( h + p \) rather than \( 2h - p \).
This reflects about \( x = 0 \) instead of about \( x = [[h]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
